decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1572, Potosí. Viceroy Francisco de Toledo must decide whether to close the Lima mint and consolidate all Spanish silver coinage at a brand-new mint built directly at Potosí, right next to the silver source — an enormous logistical gamble, since Potosí was a remote, high-altitude mining town with none of Lima's existing infrastructure, skilled labor, or administrative systems. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: the new mint could fail to reach production targets, straining the Spanish crown's finances at a critical period of imperial expansion.Player choice: Build the new mint at Potosí, or keep coinage centralized at the established Lima mint.Historical resolution: Toledo built at Potosí. The mint operated continuously from 1573/1574 until 1773, becoming the source of the coinage that dominated world trade for two centuries.

understand.economic_impact: The Mexican peso's silver became the working capital of a genuinely global trading system for the first time in history — Chinese silk and porcelain flowed to Mexico via Manila, and Mexican-minted silver flowed back to China to settle the trade balance, for roughly 250 years Manila Galleon feature. The coin's design and reliability directly shaped the eventual U.S. dollar Smithsonian object page.
understand.political_impact: The mint's founding inside Moctezuma II's confiscated palace was a direct assertion of political conquest through currency — money itself became a tool of colonial replacement Wikipedia: Antonio de Mendoza. The mita forced-labor system that extracted Potosí's silver was one of the most consequential and lethal labor institutions of the Spanish colonial era, and it is now formally preserved as part of a UNESCO World Heritage designation precisely because of this history UNESCO: City of Potosí.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Before Camila's coin, there wasn't really a "world economy" — different regions traded with each other in small, disconnected ways. Her coin changed that. Silver mined by forced laborers in the mountains of Bolivia was turned into coins in Mexico City, shipped across the Pacific Ocean to the Philippines, and used to buy silk and tea directly from China — and it worked because everyone, everywhere, trusted that a Mexican peso was really made of the silver it claimed to be. That's the same reason you trust a $20 bill today: not because of the paper, but because everyone agrees to believe in it together. And the story has a hard truth in it too — that trust was built on the backs of people who never chose to be part of it, working in brutal conditions in the mountains, with none of the wealth their labor created ever coming back to them.
